# Healthy ageing

> Old age flips the usual intuition. Appetite fades and the plate gets smaller, yet the need for
> protein and most nutrients does *not* fall with it, and one need actually goes **up**. The advice an
> older body needs is almost never "eat less"; it is eat denser, keep your muscle, and, above all,
> don't eat alone. Much of the clinician's job is to anticipate these shifts and to recognise the
> moment a normal one has tipped into something to refer.

## Healthy ageing at a glance: the two drivers

This is the last chapter of Part II, covering the older adult (roughly **65 and over**), ageing *in
health*, after the *Adulthood: working life & activity* and *Menopause & Midlife Health* chapters. As
everywhere in this part, the story runs on two drivers, and here they pull in an unexpected direction.

- **What's changing inside**, the *internal* drivers: appetite and energy needs **fall**, but the need
  for protein and micronutrients per calorie **rises**; ageing muscle answers protein more weakly; and
  B12, vitamin D and thirst all quietly decline.
- **What's changing outside**, the *external* levers: **who the older person eats with**. Widowhood,
  living alone, and the practical struggle to shop and cook do more damage to an older diet than any single
  nutrient, and a shared meal is one of the strongest remedies.

The one idea to carry through the chapter is a mismatch: **energy down, density up**. The interactive makes
it concrete: watch the needs diverge with age, then rebuild a real local meal to meet them.

<div data-island="same-plate" data-src="/diagrams/same-plate.yml"></div>

## Energy down, density up

Two things happen at once, and they point in opposite directions. Appetite and food intake genuinely
**fall** with age, the "anorexia of ageing," so an older adult eats meaningfully less than they did as a
young adult [@giezenaar2016appetite]. But the recommendations for **protein and most vitamins and minerals
do not fall in step**; the same nutrients now have to be met from fewer calories
[@espenGeriatrics2019]. The whole chapter is contained in those four words, **energy down, density up**:
the right response is a *more nutrient-dense* plate, not a smaller version of the same one. Advising an
older adult to "just eat less of everything" gets it exactly backwards.

## Protein: the one need that goes up

The clearest reversal of intuition is protein. Healthy older adults need **more** protein than younger
ones, on the order of **1.0–1.2 g per kilogram per day** versus the 0.8 g/kg adult reference intake set in
the *Macronutrients: Carbohydrate, Protein, Fat & Fibre* chapter (a figure to illustrate the *upward shift*,
not a dose to titrate) [@bauer2013protage; @deutz2014espen]. The reason is **anabolic resistance**: ageing
muscle mounts a weaker building response to a given amount of protein, so it takes *more* to get the same
effect [@breen2011anabolic]. Two practical consequences follow, and, as in the *Adulthood* chapter, protein
only works **paired with the stimulus of exercise**, especially resistance work [@deutz2014espen]:

- **Spread it across the day.** Because each meal has to clear a higher bar to trigger muscle-building,
  banking all the protein at dinner is less effective than **a palm-sized portion of protein at each meal**
  [@mamerow2014distribution]. Treat that as a sensible rule of thumb grounded in the anabolic-resistance
  mechanism rather than a precise dose, since the outcome trials in older adults are mixed.
- **It defends against sarcopenia.** Sarcopenia is the age-related loss of muscle **strength and mass**.
  It is screened simply in the community, where a weak hand grip is a common first clue, using cut-offs set
  lower for Asian builds, and then **referred**, with diagnosis and management belonging to later clinical
  training [@cruzjentoft2019ewgsop2; @chen2020awgs2019; @limSarcopeniaCpg2022]. Why it matters now: losing
  muscle strength, together with the deficiency-prone vitamin D below, raises the risk of **falls and
  fractures**, the downstream harm that makes getting protein and activity right early worthwhile
  [@roberts2021community; @neo2016vitDsg].

## The micronutrient and hydration watch-list

A few nutrients need specific attention in later life. What follows is the age-specific *shift*, not the
underlying biology (which is the *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapter's).

- **Vitamin B12, a problem of absorption, not intake.** With age the stomach makes less acid (atrophic
  gastritis), and B12 can no longer be split from food protein to be absorbed, so deficiency comes from
  **malabsorption** rather than a lack of B12 in the diet [@andres2005foodCobalamin]. Long-term **metformin and
  proton-pump inhibitors** add to the risk, and both are common in Singapore's diabetes-prevalent older population
  [@porter2021b12Gastritis; @metforminB12]. The message is to **watch the at-risk**: atrophic gastritis, those
  drugs, or unexplained anaemia or nerve/memory changes, and check or refer rather than screen everyone
  [@mouchaileh2023b12Older].
- **Vitamin D, the sunny-country paradox.** The classic teaching is that ageing skin makes vitamin D less
  efficiently [@maclaughlin1985skinVitD], though newer work questions how large that effect really is
  [@borecka2024skin7dhc]. What is robust is the *behavioural* risk: older adults spend less time outdoors
  and cover up, so deficiency is common **even in sunny Singapore** [@cheong2020shield]. Dosing and the
  bone evidence belong to the *Bone & Joint Health* chapter.
- **Calcium and hydration.** Calcium needs rise as absorption becomes less efficient, a wide local gap
  best met **food-first** and otherwise referred to the bone chapter [@ye2023daha]. And **thirst is
  blunted** in older adults, who dehydrate before they feel thirsty; the everyday bedside signs of
  dehydration are unreliable in the old, so the lever is to **offer fluids proactively** rather than wait
  for thirst [@li2023hydrationOlder; @hooper2016drieDehydration].

## Why older adults eat less, and what to do

The falling appetite is real and multi-causal: the stomach empties more slowly and signals fullness sooner,
and **taste and (especially) smell fade**, draining food of its reward [@wysokinski2015anorexia]. On top of
that sit three practical, checkable problems.

- **Teeth.** Poor dentition pushes people toward soft, carbohydrate-heavy food and away from meat, fruit and
  vegetables. *Can this patient chew a piece of meat?* [@chan2023oralHealth]
- **Swallowing.** Difficulty swallowing is common, and although texture-modified food and thickened fluids
  keep it *safe*, they tend to **lower** intake, so enrich them and refer the red flags: coughing on food
  or drink, a wet voice, recurrent chest infections [@wu2020tmd; @espenGeriatricsPractical2022].
- **Medicines.** Taking **five or more** contributes to poor appetite, nausea and altered taste
  [@morleySilver1995meals], a prompt to flag polypharmacy for review [@kok2022polypharmacy].

Loss of appetite is often an **early** sign, appearing before weight is lost, so it is worth noticing
[@desoutobarreto2022icfsr]. The classic *MEALS ON WHEELS* checklist is a useful memory aid for the
reversible causes of an older person's weight loss [@morleySilver1995meals].

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Fortify the familiar
The first move for a frail older adult who eats little is not a supplement drink. It is **making the food
they already eat more nourishing**. Stir an **egg, minced fish, silken tofu, ikan bilis or milk powder**
into the morning porridge or *mee sua*; enrich soups; choose the **yong tau foo** pieces that are tofu and
fish rather than deep-fried; put a palm-sized protein at every meal. This "food-first" fortification
reliably raises energy and protein **without adding volume**, which is ideal when a small appetite limits how
much goes on the plate [@geny2023fortification; @mills2018fortified]. And because smell and taste have faded,
**amplify flavour** with herbs, spice and umami rather than stripping it away, one reason *not* to put a thin,
frail elder on a bland "healthy" low-salt diet [@schiffman1993flavor].
:::

## The social plate

For older adults, the most powerful nutrition lever is often not a nutrient at all. It is **company**.
People eat **more, and more variedly, in the company of familiar others** than alone
[@ruddock2019facilitation]. So **eating alone is a practical red flag**: solo eaters have poorer, less
varied diets, and more frequent solitary eating tracks with lower fruit, vegetable and protein intake and
more low mood [@bjornwall2024eatingAlone; @kwon2023eatingAlone]. **Social isolation** in particular, the
objective lack of contact, predicts inadequate micronutrient intake [@steptoe2024isolation]. Three
practical points follow. **Widowhood** is a discrete danger window for weight loss and lost interest in
food, worth checking proactively [@shahar2001widowhood]. **Food insecurity in the old is about access as
much as money**: *can you get to the shops, carry the groceries, stand to cook?* [@tucher2021foodInsecurity]
And **communal meal programmes work**, raising intake and reaching exactly the isolated people who need them
[@middleton2022sharedMeal; @cpstf2021meals]. "Who do you eat with?" is one of the highest-yield questions in
a geriatric diet history.

## When healthy ageing tips into a problem

Everything so far is *ageing in health*. The signal that it has become a clinical problem is simple and
must not be dismissed as "just getting old": **unintentional weight loss of about 5% or more over six to
twelve months**, corroborated by looser clothes or a relative's account, warrants evaluation and
**referral** [@gaddey2021weightLoss]. It is also the first item of the frailty picture, alongside weakness,
slow walking and exhaustion [@fried2001frailty]. At this point the older adult has crossed into
**undernutrition**, and the formal screening tools, the multidisciplinary team, oral supplement drinks and
tube feeding all belong to the *Undernutrition & Malnutrition* chapter. Here the task is to **recognise and
refer**.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do no harm: don't put a frail, thin elder on a "diet"
The reflex to prescribe a low-fat, low-salt, low-sugar or weight-loss diet, sensible for a younger adult,
is actively **harmful** in a frail, underweight older person: it strips intake and flavour and can tip them
into malnutrition. Dietary restriction should generally be **avoided** in older age; weight-loss diets are
reserved for the *obese* older adult with weight-related problems, and always paired with exercise
[@espenGeriatrics2019]. In a thin older adult who is losing weight, the rule is **density up, restriction
off**: support intake, and refer.
:::

## The Singapore picture

Singapore is ageing fast. The share of citizens over 65 has climbed steeply and is heading toward **one in
four**, which makes this chapter's skills increasingly everyday [@sgPopulationLongevity]. The local anchors
map cleanly onto the drivers above.

**The HPB Dietary Guidelines for Older Adults** are the primary scaffold: **nutrient density over quantity,
more protein, calcium-rich foods, softer textures, and more fluids**, the "energy down, density up" message
in national form [@hpbSeniors]. Local guidance puts numbers to the protein point, roughly **1.2 g/kg a day
for over-50s, about a palm-sized protein at each of three meals**, carried by familiar foods (eggs, a palm
of fish, tofu, pulses) [@healthhubSeniorsProtein]. Yet local survey data suggest **about one in two older
adults fall short of their protein target** [@mohNNS2022]. The practical response is the **🍜 "fortify the
familiar"** move above: build protein and energy into porridge, soup and *yong tau foo* rather than
prescribing unfamiliar "diet foods."

The internal watch-list has a distinctly local shape. **Vitamin-D deficiency is common despite the sun**,
around half of healthy community seniors have low levels, driven by indoor, sun-avoidant living rather than
latitude [@cheong2020shield]; and **calcium falls short in about half of older adults**, a large fraction well
below target [@ye2023daha]. B12, reassuringly, is uncommon in *well* local seniors, so reserve testing
for the at-risk.

The sharpest local lesson is on the **external** driver. In Singapore's multigenerational households, a
striking finding is that many older adults **eat alone even though they live with family**: adult children
are out at work, and the elder eats by themselves [@ye2023daha]. So *living with family does not guarantee a
shared meal*, and here it is **loneliness**, more than living arrangement on paper, that tracks with frailty
[@geSocialFrailtySg2022]. The referral endpoints are local: **Active Ageing Centres** for communal dining and
befriending, and the wider **Age Well SG** programme, the connections a clinician can make when the diet
history reveals an older person eating alone [@mohAgeWellSg2023].

## When to refer, the quick list

Any of the following, drawn from the sections above, is a prompt to recognise and refer:

| Trigger | What to look for |
|---|---|
| **Unintentional weight loss** | ~5%+, or looser clothes |
| **Eating alone / newly widowed / isolated** | an older adult eating alone, recently bereaved, or without regular contact |
| **Swallowing red flags** | coughing on food or fluids, a wet voice, recurrent chest infections |
| **Suspected sarcopenia** | a weak grip, difficulty rising from a chair |
| **Restrictive "diet" in a frail elder** | a low-fat, low-salt, low-sugar or weight-loss diet in a thin, frail older adult |
| **At-risk B12 profile** | atrophic gastritis, long-term metformin/PPI |

## Is old age just eating less?

No, and that misreading does real harm. Almost everything about later-life nutrition runs *against* the
"eat less" reflex: energy needs fall but **nutrient density must rise**; protein needs go **up**, not down,
and want spreading across the day and pairing with movement; the plate should be **fortified and flavourful**,
not stripped bare. And the biggest lever is the most human one: an older person eating **in company**
rather than alone. The clinician's job is to anticipate those shifts, make the small food-first moves, and
recognise the one clear signal, unintentional weight loss, that means it is time to **refer**, since the
disease end of this spectrum is the *Undernutrition & Malnutrition* chapter's to manage.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/healthy-ageing.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: the widow who eats alone

Mdm Lim is 78, widowed four months ago, and lives in an HDB flat. Her daughter, who works full-time,
notices her *cheongsam* hangs loose, that she "can't be bothered to cook for one," and often skips lunch.
She takes long-term metformin and a stomach-acid tablet. Work through the visit: notice that the tempting
"healthy diet" advice is the wrong one, that the loosening clothes are the real signal, and that your job is
to lift her intake, reconnect her to a shared meal, and refer. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/mdm-lim-widowed.case.yml"></div>
